title: Overview of statistical methods for adding admin records to the 2018 Census dataset | Stats NZ
content:
## Overview of statistical methods for adding admin records to the 2018 Census dataset
23 September 2019, 11:00am
Overview of statistical methods for adding administrative records to the 2018 Census dataset describes the statistical methods used to determine high-quality administrative (admin) records to include in the 2018 Census dataset.
Download the PDF document or read the summary of key points online.
- Overview of statistical methods for adding admin records to the 2018 census dataset Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 2.6 MB

### Overview of statistical methods for adding admin records to the 2018 census dataset
Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 2.6 MB

## Summary of key points
For the first time, the New Zealand 2018 Census dataset includes administrative (admin) records to include people who were missed by the census field collection, replacing the use of ‘substitute’ imputed records in previous censuses. The 2018 Census dataset is made up of census forms received through field collection, supplemented by high-quality administrative records for people from whom we have not received a response. These admin enumerations are drawn from a New Zealand resident population that has been derived from administrative data. Members of this New Zealand admin resident population are only added to the census dataset if they were in New Zealand on census night, and have not already responded to the census.
Administrative records are included as usual residents in a private dwelling when the administrative household membership information is reliable. In two cases (prisons and defence establishments) administrative records contribute to non-private dwellings. Otherwise, administrative records are not placed in dwellings, but have been counted within the smallest geographic area (a meshblock, up to about 100 people, which serves as a building block for all standard geographies) when evidence suggested this would improve the quality of census small-area data. Some potential administrative records are not included due to limitations in identifying individuals’ location. Statistical methods underpin each of these steps and are designed to account for limitations in the quality of administrative sources.
Our understanding of the strengths and limitations of the admin resident population builds on previous work carried out through Stats NZ’s Census Transformation programme. The main limitations of the administrative data are some undercoverage and overcoverage relative to the true population, and the reliability of administrative addresses. To overcome these limitations, we developed statistical models to predict how reliable the administrative data are for representing an entire household, and a related model predicts the likelihood that the administrative meshblock reflects a person’s true usual residence meshblock. Before considering the administrative records for inclusion in meshblocks, we undertake two main adjustments to ensure that we are not introducing people to the census file who should not be included. We first adjust for potential overcoverage in the admin population. The second adjustment is to account for duplication caused by missed linkages between the received census forms and the administrative data.
The final 2018 Census dataset consists of 89 percent census responses and 11 percent admin enumerations. Admin enumerations make up almost a quarter of the Māori and Pacific populations in the final 2018 Census dataset, compared with 10 percent for the European ethnic group, an indication of lower participation for people of Māori and Pacific ethnicity in the 2018 Census.
The careful use of administrative data has enabled Stats NZ to add real data about real people to the 2018 Census dataset where we are sure they should have been counted but we have not received a census form. The inclusion of admin enumerations also removes the need to add missing census records through statistical imputation processes.
